caption: Pre-spin Aviation snapshot: $22B '20 revenue, 37K+ commercial / 26K+ military engines, services > equipment; through-cycle targets of high-teens-to-20% margin and 90%+ FCF conversion -- the franchise GE would become.

The aftermarket annuity mechanism: ~50% of CFM56 engines had not seen a first shop visit and ~60% of widebodies no 2nd SV; workscope deepens each shop visit -- the razor-and-blade that seeds decades of services.
Commercial services revenue trajectory: $15B (2019) collapsed to $8-9B in COVID, with shop-visit volume >25% growth in '22 -- quantifies the aftermarket recovery slope coming out of the pandemic.
Installed-base map at the spin: ~41K commercial engines (largest, youngest fleet) + ~26K defense; ~70% services for commercial, >70% for defense -- the structural asymmetry between the two segments.

Shop-visit economics decoded: workscope by module, SV1/SV2/SV3 timing (~5-10/10-15/15-20+ yrs), and how the contract type migrates from CSA to T&M/external as engines age -- the per-unit aftermarket grammar.

CFM56 the cash engine: ~23K engines, nearly half of -5B/7B yet to see first shop visit, retirement age pushed to ~22 yrs; SV forecast revised UP '21->'22 -- the mature-fleet annuity that funds the LEAP ramp.

LEAP profitability gating items: shop-visit volume, durability (time-on-wing below customer expectation at maturity but ahead of CFM56 at same age), and unit cost -- program profitable & OE breakeven only by mid-decade.

LEAP durability fix roadmap: shroud ('18), RDS ('19), then HPT blade & nozzle and fuel-nozzle redesigns ramping into production '23-'25 -- the named engineering items behind the aftermarket-margin story.

Standalone GE Aerospace at launch: $32B '23 revenue, ~70% services; CES $23.9B at ~70% services vs DPT $9.0B at ~55% services -- the two-segment economic split and the services-mix gap that drives the margin gap.

Through-cycle compounding: revenue 3x and operating profit 3x from 2000->2023 ($10.8B->$31.8B; $2.0B->$6.1B), installed base +60% to 70K -- the multi-decade earnings power of the installed-base annuity.

The commercial program flywheel: ~3.5x services revenue follows each fielded engine over 20+ years; SV1/2/3 each ~20/30/25% of lifecycle revenue with contract type shifting CSA->T&M -- the core economic engine.

CES platform-revenue mix: ~50% narrowbody / ~35% widebody / ~15% regional & other on ~44,000 engines, '23-'28 revenue CAGR low-double-digits -- where the commercial profit pool actually sits by airframe class.

Narrowbody share moat: ~60% LEAP-1A life-of-program win rate on A320, sole-source LEAP-1B on 737MAX; CFM56 the industry's most-utilized engine (1.2B flight hours, ~19K in service, ~45% yet to first SV).

CFM56->LEAP transition mechanics: LEAP installed base +MSD CAGR and services revenue +LDD CAGR '23-'28 as LEAP overtakes CFM56; profit mix flips toward LEAP -- the single most important multi-year driver.
Widebody share moat: ~7,200 GE engines on world widebodies; win rates ~75% GEnx/787, ~60% GE9X-vs-A350; >80% of GEnx not yet at first SV -- the next-decade widebody aftermarket runway.

LEAP OE ramp: deliveries 815(2022)->1,136(2023)->~1,700 mid-decade plus a parallel shop-visit ramp where external & CBSA shops grow to ~20-25% -- the twin OE+aftermarket ramp and the network build-out it forces.

Durability as a designed-in advantage: removal-rate per 1,000 hrs improving GE90->GEnx->LEAP across generations, with each new program seeded by prior-fleet learnings -- the technical basis for the reliability claim.

CFM RISE open-fan rationale: open fan reaches ~20% fuel-burn reduction at the same core temperature a ducted engine cannot, lowering technical risk -- the engineering logic for the next-gen narrowbody roadmap.

Defense platform map & backlog: F404/F414/F110 combat, T700/T408/T901 rotorcraft, LM2500 marine across U.S. & international fleets; $11.5B Defense RPO = 2.4x '23 revenue, 1.2x book-to-bill -- the DPT order pipeline.

LEAP cash inflection: CSA billings ~3x and shop visits ~2x from '24->'28, billings outpacing SV growth at ~30% CAGR, LEAP profit exceeds CFM56 by 2030 -- when the LEAP fleet turns from cash drain to cash engine.

Pricing power evidence: price set to outrun inflation through '25 with MSD price increases, and realized engine pricing (GEnx, LEAP) stepped up materially post-EIS pre-'18 to '22-'23 -- the financial fingerprint of the moat.

Capital-allocation framework: ~$34-36B available '24-'26 (FCF + $25B equity stakes + cash), 70-75% returned to holders, $15B buyback, dividend 30% of net income; R&D 6-8% & capex ~3% of revenue -- the standalone playbook.

Original 2028 framework set at the spin: HSD revenue CAGR '25-'28 and ~$10B operating profit with ~100% FCF conversion -- the baseline target later beaten, useful as the yardstick for the post-spin track record.

First full year as standalone: CES FY'24 revenue +13%, op profit $7.1B (+25%), margin +250bps; 4Q services +12% with internal shop visits (3)% -- early proof the services-led margin model works post-spin.

Tariff & demand-management response (1Q'25): ~$0.5B tariff cost to be offset via pricing & SG&A control, FY departures cut to +LSD from +MSD with a slower 2H -- the first crack in the post-spin demand story.

Supply-constrained ramp quantified (FY'25): internal shop-visit revenue +24%, spare parts >25%, LEAP deliveries record 1,802 (+28%, guided +15% in '26); >$1B MRO investment incl ~$0.5B for LEAP capacity.

Raised long-term framework: FY'26 guide $9.85-10.25B op profit on +LDD revenue, with '24-'26 CAGRs of mid-teens revenue, mid-twenties adj. EPS, mid-teens FCF -- targets well above the 2024 Investor-Day baseline.

2026 segment recast: CES Services moves ~$0.8B and the aeroderivative business shifts CES->DPT, lifting DPT revenue +$1.7B and trimming CES; the FY2025 segment splits restate -- a reporting-quirk trap for trend lines.
Current inflection (1Q'26): Middle East departures forecast slashed from +HSD (Jan) to -LDD (Apr), global departures cut to flat/+LSD; mgmt invokes the 2008 analog (services lagged RPK by 12 months).
